coredata.dc:title: Promising photodynamic antimicrobial activity of polyimine substituted zinc phthalocyanine and its polycationic derivative when conjugated to nitrogen, sulfur, co-doped graphene quantum dots against Staphylococcus aureus
coredata.dc:creator: Sen, Pinar
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Antimicrobial resistance is a most important problem facing the treatment of infectious diseases. Antimicrobial photodynamic therapy is an alternative treatment strategy, considered to be cost-effective and feasible. For this purpose, octa-imine substituted ZnPc (3) have been prepared and conjugated to nitrogen, sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) through π-π stacking. The photophysical and photochemical properties of Pc alone and and Pc-conjugated to the GQD nanomaterial such as absorption, fluorescence, fluorescence life time, singlet oxygen quantum yields, triplet state quantum yields and exited state lifetimes were investigated in solutions before in vitro cell studies. The PACT activity of prepared structures was investigated against Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus). Our results suggest that the in the case of conjugation of zinc Pc to N,S-GQDs, photodynamic inactivation increased with the 100 % reduction percentage
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc phthalocyanines
Schiff base
Graphene quantum dots
Photodynamic antimicrobial therapy
S. aureus
Photophysicochemical properties
Antimicrobial photodynamic therapy

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF), emission and lifetimes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation, National Research Foundation (NRF) South Africa and Sasol Inzalo Foundation through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology ( UID 62620 ) as well as Rhodes University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis route: (i) ethanol, reflux temperature, (ii) DMF, NaH, 60 °C, (iii) n-pentanol, DBU, ZnCl2, (iv) DMF, CH3I.
Schematic illustration of conjugation ZnPc 3 (as an example) to N,S-GQDs.
Absorption spectra of the Pcs (3,4) in DMSO (Concentration 1 × 10−5 mol.L−1).
Absorption spectra of the Pc (3), 3@N,S-GQDs in DMSO and N,S-GQDs in water (a), Pc (4) and 4@N,S-GQDs in DMSO and in aq (5%DMSO/water) (b).
X-ray diffraction patterns of ZnPcs (3,4) and 3@N,S-GQDs, 4@N,S-GQDs compared to N,S-GQDs alone.
Raman spectra of N,S-GQDs, 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs showing the corresponding Raman intensity changes of the N,S-GQDs upon conjugation to Pcs 3 and 4.
DLS graph showing average particle size for the N,S-GQDs alone and when conjugated to Pcs (3@N,S-GQDs, 4@N,S-GQDs).
TEM images of N,S-GQDs alone (A), 3@N,S-GQDs (B), 4@N,S-GQDs (C) and size distribution plot for N,S-GQDs. TEM scale = 100 nm.
EDX spectra for N,S-GQDs alone, ZnPcs (3,4) and 3@N,S-GQDs, 4@N,S-GQDs.
Normalized absorption, excitation and emission spectra of ZnPc 3 (Excitation wavelength: 610 nm, emission wavelength: 695).
Fluorescence decay (blue), χ2 ﬁtting (black) and IRF (red) curves for complex3 in DMSO.

(a) Representative absorption spectral changes during the determination of singlet oxygen quantum yield of 3 in DMSO using DPBF, (b) Change in absorbance of DPBF (at 416 nm) versus irradiation time in the presence of 3,4, 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs, (c) Representative absorption spectral changes during the determination of singlet oxygen quantum yield of 4@N,S-GQDs in aqueous media (5% DMSO) using ADMA, (d)) Change in absorbance of ADMA (at 357 nm) versus irradiation time in the presence of 4 and 4@N,S-GQDs.
Triplet absorption decay (black) and fitting (red) curve for complex 3 (solvent = DMSO).

(a) Phototoxicity studies S. Aureus in presence of ZnPc (3) and 3@N,S-GQD with light and in the dark (b) Survival percentage of S. aureus of in presence of ZnPc (3) and 3@N,S-GQD with light and in the dark.
Agar plates micrographs depicting the S. Aureus colonies after treatment with 5% DMSO in PBS as control (a), 3@N,S-GQD (b) after 80 min irridation.
Photophysicochemical parameters of the studied compounds.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are for excitation at the wavelength where GQDs absorb.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets DLS sizes in nm/loading of the Pc onto the N,S-GQDs (mg/mg).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Logarithmic and percentage cell reduction values of S.aureus after 80 min irradiation in the presence of photosensitizers.

head.ce:title: Promising photodynamic antimicrobial activity of polyimine substituted zinc phthalocyanine and its polycationic derivative when conjugated to nitrogen, sulfur, co-doped graphene quantum dots against Staphylococcus aureus

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Grahamstown, 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Grahamstown, 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antimicrobial resistance is a most important problem facing the treatment of infectious diseases. Antimicrobial photodynamic therapy is an alternative treatment strategy, considered to be cost-effective and feasible. For this purpose, octa-imine substituted ZnPc (3) have been prepared and conjugated to nitrogen, sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) through π-π stacking. The photophysical and photochemical properties of Pc alone and and Pc-conjugated to the GQD nanomaterial such as absorption, fluorescence, fluorescence life time, singlet oxygen quantum yields, triplet state quantum yields and exited state lifetimes were investigated in solutions before in vitro cell studies. The PACT activity of prepared structures was investigated against Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus). Our results suggest that the in the case of conjugation of zinc Pc to N,S-GQDs, photodynamic inactivation increased with the 100 % reduction percentage

ce:section.ce:para: The work in the domain of antimicrobials is one of the most important health-related issues worldwide. Pathogenic microorganisms widely exist everywhere such as in the air, on different surfaces and in food [1]. Unfortunately, antibiotics used for public health to inactivate the microorganisms are not always effective due to the resistance of bacteria to current antibiotics [2]. Moreover, some conventional methods for disinfection of surfaces and foods such as ultraviolet (UV) irradiation and thermotherapy require excessive amounts of energy [3,4]. These drawbacks limit the applicability of the current methods.

The need to overcome these deficiencies has stimulated scientists to develop alternative treatments and antimicrobial reagents towards microbial infections. Recently, research efforts have been addressed to light-based technology called photodynamic therapy (PDT) [5]. PDT was originally applied as an anticancer method and is currently clinically utilized in the treatment of cancer [6].

The photodynamic inactivation process is based on the use of visible light in combination with photoactive molecule called as photosensitizer (PS) in the presence of molecular oxygen in and around cells [7]. After the PSs are exposed to illumination, they are excited to the singlet state and can switch between systems to transition to the triple state (3PS*) where they form singlet oxygen which is toxic for the biomolecules [8].

In this context, photodynamic antimicrobial therapy (PACT) has been recognized as a bright approach to efficiently destroy microorganisms and has shown good results [9,10]. The photosensitizer is administered and selectively accumulates in the microorganisms, followed by irradiation with visible light. The formed toxic oxygen species cause the cell inactivation and death through selective destruction of the target microorganism [11]. It is obvious that it is difficult for bacteria to develop resistance against PACT.

The PSs play a precious role in PACT since the efficacy of the photodynamic action depends on the efficiency of singlet oxygen generation by the PSs. So far, some kinds of photosensitizing drugs including porphyrins, chlorins, bacteriochlorins with different molecular frameworks have been tested in vitro and in vivo against pathogens [12–14].

Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are valuable photosensitizer because they have the desired physicochemical properties. They can form long-lived triplet excited states, consequently they show high singlet oxygen production as well as having intracellular localization [15].

The PACT activity of phthalocyanines has been studied against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [16,17]. In order to obtain suitable Pcs, the design of the substituents is important. The Pcs existing the positively charged are more suitable against the microorganisms in terms of displaying better solubility and improved cell uptake [18,19].

The Schiff bases (imine) are of great importance due to pharmacological and biological properties such as antitumour, antioxidant, anti-bacterial, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, tyrosinase inhibitory activities [20–23]. However, phthalocyanines carrying imine functional group substituents are rare [24,25]. Schiff base substituted Indium phthalocyanines (positively charged) have been used for PACT against E. Coli in the presence of Ag/Fe3O4 dimers and core shell nanoparticles [26]. In this work we use new Schiff base substituted Zn phthalocyanines conjugated to graphene quantum dots.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a class of carbon material with a single-layer carbon core which have substituents on the edges for functionalisation [27]. GQDs are well known PDT agents with superior properties showing, biocompatibility and low toxicity which are desirable [28].
These properties have enabled them to be used in biomedical applications such as biosensing, drug delivery and photosensitizers in PDT [29–31].
The conjugation of Pcs to GQDs could be provided by non-covalently via π-π stacking since the delocalized electrons of graphene interact with other π-conjugated structures [32].

Doping for modification of GQDs is an effective method to change their optical properties. Only a limited number of nitrogen, sulfur co-doped GQDs (N,S-GQDs) studies have been reported [33,34], since the doping with sulfur atom is relatively difficult in comparison with other dopants because of its electronegativity [35]. Pc-conjugate with N,S-GQDs for PACT studies have not been reported yet.

In this research study, in order to enhance the photodynamic activity of synthesized Pcs, we combine the two PDT agents (N,S-GQDs and Pc) through a synergistic effect. The photophysical-chemical properties of the Pcs alone and Pc-conjugated to the N,S-GQD were determined. The photodynamic antimicrobial studies were carried out against Staphylococcus aureus by comparing with Pcs alone and Pcs in the presence of N,S-GQDs.

4-(Dimethylamino)benzaldehyde, 1,3-diamino-2-propanol, NaH, anhydrous ZnCl2, 1,8-diazabicyclo [5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU), n-pentanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methyl iodide (CH3I), dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (EtOH), chloroform (CHCl3), diethyl ether, acetone, ethylacetate, citric acid, thiourea, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) and 4-nitrophthalonitrile were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All solvents were dried as reported by Perrin and Armarego [36] before using. Silica gel (Merck grade 60) was used at column chromatography. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution pH 7.4 was prepared using appropriate amounts of Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4 in ultra-pure water from a Millipore water was from ELGA, Veolia water PURELAB, flex system (Marlow, UK). Nutrient agar and agar bacteriological BBL Muller Hinton broth were purchased from Merck, S. aureus (ATCC 25,923) were obtained Davies Diagnostics.

Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse spectrofluorometer with quartz cell of 1 cm at room temperature. Infrared spectra were recorded on a Bruker ALPHA FT-IR spectrometer with platinum attenuated total reflectance (ATR) sampling accessory. Absorption spectra were recorded using on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 300, Picoquant GmbH). The excitation source was a diode laser (LDH-P-670 driven by PDL 800-B, 670 nm, 20 MHz repetition rate, 44 ps pulse width, Picoquant GmbH). Triplet quantum yield values were obtained using a laser flash photolysis system having a LP980 spectrometer with a PMT-LP detector and an ICCD camera (Andor DH320T-25F03). The signal from a PMT detector was recorded on a Tektronix TDS3012C digital storage oscilloscope. The excitation pulses were produced by a tunable laser system consisting of a Nd:YAG laser (355 nm, 135 mJ/4–6 ns), pumping an optical parametric oscillator (OPO, 30 mJ/3–5 ns) with a 420–2300 nm (NT-342B, Ekspla) wavelength range. Acquisition of 1H NMR spectra was carried out on a Bruker AMX600 MHz NMR spectrometer. Mass analysis was performed on a Bruker AutoFLEX III Smartbeam TOF/TOF Mass spectrometer. Photo-irradiations for singlet oxygen studies were done using a General Electric Quartz line lamp (300 W). A 600 nm glass cut off filter (Schott) and a water filter were used to filter off ultraviolet and infrared radiations respectively. An interference filter (Intor, 670 nm with a band width of 40 nm) was additionally placed in the light path before the sample. Light intensity was measured with a POWER MAX5100 (Molelectron detector incorporated) power meter. The Modulight ML7710-680-RHO laser system (680 wavelength probe) was employed for PACT studies. For PACT studies various irradiation periods at 0.5 W cm−2 irradiance, resulting in energy doses ranging from 0.9 kJ cm−2 to 3.6 kJ cm−2 corresponding to time periods ranging from 15 min to 60 min were employed. Raman spectra were obtained from a Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer equipped with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and a liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector. Size determination using dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed using a Malvern Zetasizer nanoseries, Nano-ZS90. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected over the 2θ = 10–100° range, scanning at 0.010° min − 1 and 192 s per step. A zero-background silicon wafer slide was used and the measurements were carried out using a Bruker® D8 Discover diffractometer, equipped with a Lynx Eye detector, under Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å).

Compound (1) was synthesized according to literature [37] with slight modification. 4-(Dimethylamino)benzaldehyde (1 g, 6.7 mmoL) was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol, 1,3-diamino-2-propanol (0.3 g, 3.35 mmoL) was added to the solution in presence of catalytic amount of acetic acid. The reaction mixture was stirred at reflux temperature under nitrogen for 8 h. The reaction was controlled by thin layer chromatography (TLC) (CHCl3/EtOH 10/0.1). After the reaction completion, the solution was brought to room temperature and the precipitate formed was filtered off, washed with ethanol and dried in vacuum desiccators. Yield: 80 % (0.95 g). FT-IR (UATR-TWO™) ν max/cm−1: 3170 (OH), 3015 (Ar, C–H), 2917−2822 (Aliph., C–H), 1637 (C = N), 1600−1520, 1478−1357, 1285, 1226, 1178, 1162, 1070, 808. 1H-NMR (DMSO) δ ppm: 8.13 (s, 2H, imine), 7.56 (d, 4 H), 6.72 (d, 4 H), 4.67 (s, 1H, OH), 3.94−3.90 (m, 1 H), 3.66 (d, 2 H), 3.45 (d, 2 H), 2.96 (s, 12 H). 13C-NMR (DMSO) δ ppm: 161.79, 152.21, 129.66, 124.66, 111.93, 70.98, 65.86, 40.28

Compound (1) (0.9 g, 2.6 mmoL) was reacted with 4-nitrophthalonitrile (0.45 g, 2.6 mmol) by dissolving in DMF (40 mL) in presence of NaH (0.125 g, 5.2 mmoL). The reaction was kept at 60 °C for 1 day under nitrogen. The reaction was controlled by thin layer chromatography (TLC) (CHCl3/EtOH 10/0.1). The product was poured into ice-water mixture and the formed precipitate was filtered off. The crude product was washed with hot water, ethanol, and dried in vacuum desiccators. Yield: 75 % (0.915 g). FT-IR (UATR-TWO™) ν max/cm−1: 3078, 3041, 2917, 2227 (CN), 1636, 1593−1524, 1486−1359, 1249 (Ar-O-R), 1164, 1123, 1087, 814. 1H-NMR (DMSO) δ ppm: 8.15 (s, 2 H), 7.91 (d, 1 H), 7.81 (s, 1 H), 7.68 (d, 1 H), 7.49 (d, 4 H), 6.69 (d, 4 H), 3.97 (d, 2 H), 3.85 (d, 2 H), 3.04 (m, 1 H), 2.95 (s, 12 H). 13C-NMR (DMSO) δ ppm: 163.15, 162.85, 152.40, 136.01, 129.74, 124.11, 121.89, 121.82, 116.71, 116.49, 111.87, 11.53, 105.88, 79.66, 62.57, 56.51, 40.23. MS (MALDI-TOF): m/z 478.62 [M]+

N,N’ –Bis (4-(dimethylamino)benzylidene) amino)propan--2-yl)oxy)phthalonitrile (2) (0.25 g, 0.52 mmol) was mixed anhydrous ZnCl2 (0.018 g, 0.13 mmoL) and catalytic amount of DBU in n-pentanol (4 mL). The mixture was stirred at 140 °C for 18 h. After cooling down, the mixture was precipitated in water/methanol (1/1) and collected by centrifugation. The crude compound was washed several times with ethanol, acetone and water. A better quality of the Pc was obtained by precipitating in organic solvents dissolved in DMF. Yield: 38 % (0,1 g). UV–vis (DMSO-1 × 10−5 M): λmax (nm) (log ε) 687 (4.55), 620 (3.94), 336 (4.64). FT-IR (UATR-TWO™) ν max/cm-1:3040, 2953, 2864, 1650, 1601−1517, 1472−1396, 1222, 1176, 1091, 1047, 949, 824. 1H-NMR (DMSO) δ (ppm): 8.94 (s, 8 H), 8.36 (d, 16 H), 7.96 (s, 4 H), 7.68 (d, 16 H), 6.80 (d, 4 H), 6.58 (d, 4 H), 3.59 (d, 16 H), 3.18 (m, 4 H), 3.05 (s, 48 H). MS (MALDI-TOF): m/z 2025.71 [M+2Na]+

ZnPc (3) (0.05 g, 0.025 mmol) was dissolved in DMF and excess CH3I (2 mL) was added to the mixture. After this time, the mixture was cold down to room temperature and added diethyl ether. The formed precipitate was seperated by centrifugation. The gathered crude compound was washed several times with CHCl3 and ethanol. The resulting quaternized ZnPc (4) was dried in vacuo. Yield: 25 % (002 g). UV–vis (DMSO-1 × 10−5 M): λmax (nm) (log ε) 685 (4.69), 618 (4.13), 357 (4.62). FT-IR (UATR-TWO™) ν max/cm-1: 3150, 2954−2863, 1642, 1597, 1469−1384, 1231, 1084, 1051, 943. 1H-NMR (DMSO) δ (ppm): 8.83 (s, 8 H), 8.32 (d, 16 H), 7.95 (s, 4 H), 7.72 (d, 16 H), 7.01 (d, 4 H), 6.79 (d, 4 H), 3.67 (d, 16 H), 3.38 (m, 4 H), 3.11 (s, 72 H). MS (MALDI-TOF): m/z 495.73 [M-5I]5+, 549.20 [M-5I+3H2O]5+

The synthesis of N,S-GQDs was performed following a literature method [38]. The ZnPcs (3,4) conjugation of N,S-GQDs based on π–π stacking was carried out according to the previously reported literature by a slight modification [39]. Briefly, the solution of 3.6 mg of N,S-GQDs (in 0.5 mL H2O) was added to the solution of ZnPcs (3,4) (7.6 mg in DMF). The mixtures were sonicated for 10 h followed the stirring at room temperature for 48 h. The mixtures were precipitated with ethanol and washed with water and ethanol. The product was dried at oven at 60 °C to obtain 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs.

The photodynamic antimicrobial studies of Pcs (3,4) and their conjugates (3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs) against Staphylococcus aureus were performed through the viable cell count method according to the previously reported literature with slight modification [40]. Firstly, stock culture of S. Aureus was grown aerobically in 6 mL of nutrient broth incubated at 37 °C on a rotary shaker (∼200 rpm) to obtain bacteria culture. The incubation was maintained until the optical density of culture recorded at 600 nm was determined to be between 0.6 and 0.8. The resulting bacterial solution was centrifuged to remove the broth, and bacterial cells were washed three times with PBS. The solution of the bacteria was prepared by diluting with PBS to obtain 10−5 M concentration bacteria solution corresponding to ∼109 colony forming units -(CFU)/mL. Pc solutions were prepared by dissolving in water containing 5% DMSO and applied in the range of 0.1–20 μM in order to determine the application concentrations of Pcs. The application concentrations of Pcs were determined as 10 μM for 3 and 0.5 μM for 4. After the preparation of each bacteria/photosensitizer suspensions at certain concentrations, each suspension was incubated in an oven equipped with a shaker for 30 min in the dark at 37 °C before plating. The 100 μL solution taken from suspension was immediately inoculated on agar plates to see the activity at 0. min without treatment. 2.5 mL of each incubated bacterial-Pc suspension were kept in the dark (dark treatment) and 2.5 mL were irradiated at the Q-band maximum of the photosensitizers in a 24 well plate for 20 min time intervals up to 80 min. After irradiation and dark treatment, 100 μL aliquots of each suspension were inoculated on agar plates. The number of colonies was then counted on each plate after 18 h of incubation at 37 °C. Also, the control treatments were performed without photosensitizer both with irradiation and in dark in order to determine the effect of light and solvents. All experiments were carried out three times. The data for CFU/mL were converted to the logarithmic form.

The reaction steps are summarized in Scheme 1 . The ligand (1) was prepared by the usual condensation reaction of the 4-(dimethylamino)benzaldehyde and 1,3-diamino-2-propanol in anhydrous ethanol at reflux temperature. The phthalonitrile derivative (2) was achieved by the reaction of the compound 1 with 4-nitrophthalonitrile in DMF using NaH. The new target phthalocyanine (3) was obtained by tetramerization of the phthalonitrile derivative 2 with the ZnCl2 salt in pentanol. Finally, the positively charged ZnPc (4) was obtained by the reaction of excess CH3I in DMF. The obtained structures were proved with the spectroscopic methods such as 1H-NMR, 13C-NMR, UV/Vis, FT-IR and MS.

The prepared ZnPcs (3,4) were then conjugated to N,S-GQDs that was obtained by using citric acid as the carbon source and thiourea as N and S sources, via π-π stacking as represented in Fig. 1 . The obtained nanocomposites were characterized by using analytical techniques such as FT-IR, UV–vis, Raman spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS), XRD and EDX spectra. The results confirmed the presence of target compounds.

In FT-IR spectrum of 1, the vibration of the carbonyl coming from the aldehyde group disappeared and imine vibration (CN) band is observed at 1637 cm−1. The FT-IR spectrum of 2 displayed the absence of OH vibration at 3170 cm−1 from the 1 and existence of new vibration bands at 2227 cm-1 and 1249 cm−1 due to CN and ArOR, respectively. The cyclisation of the phthalonitrile (2) into the corresponding Pcs (3,4) was confirmed by the absence of the nitrile (CN) peaks.
The FT-IR was utilized to examine the surface functional groups of the N,S-GQDs and the functional groups of 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs.

The N,S-GQDs exhibit OH peak at 3412 cm−1 and the broad absorption bands at 3500−2800 cm-1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of carboxy-O-H. This indicate that the existence of many hydroxyl groups on the surface of N,S-GQDs. The band at 1664 cm−1 for N,S-GQDs is attributed to CO vibration of the carboxyl group. CC and CC vibrations of benzene rings, CN vibrations of the pyridine ring, CO vibrations which were on the edge of the N,S-GQDs were easily seen in the range of 1549−1045 cm−1. The peaks at 1091 cm−1 in N,S-GQDs attribute to the CS. Upon conjugation of the ZnPcs (3,4) to N,S-GQDs, aliphatic CH peaks appeared in the range of 2980–2876 cm−1 coming from the aliphatic chain of the phthalocyanines.

The 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR data presented consistent results for studied structures. In the 1H-NMR spectra of compound 1, proton signal coing from the aldehyde compounds disappear and the peak of imine proton is observed at 8.13 ppm. The methylene protons are observed between 3.94–3.45 ppm for 1. O-H signal was observed at 4.67 ppm. The lack of OH proton signal of 1 and the coming out the new peaks in aromatic region at 7.91–7.68 ppm were the proof that the phthalonitrile derivative 2 was obtained by nitro displacement reaction.

In the 1H-NMR spectra of ZnPc (3), the aromatic protons and CHN protons were observed between 8.36–6.58 ppm and 8.94 ppm, respectively. In the 1H-NMR spectra of ZnPc (4), CHN protons were observed at 8.83 ppm and the aromatic protons were observed at 8.32−6.79 ppm. After quaternization of 3 to give 4, the methyl protons were observed at 3.11 ppm as different from 3. In the 13C-NMR spectrum of 1 the presence of the signals at 161.79 ppm belonging to the CN group is the characteristic proof for the imine formation. In the 13C-NMR spectrum of 2, 116.71 and 116.49 ppm based on the nitrile carbon atoms are clearly different from 1.

The MALDI-TOF MS data for ligand (2) and ZnPcs (3,4) were consistent with the expected structures by showing the molecular ion peaks at m/z 478.62 [M]+ for 2 and m/z 2025.71 [M+2Na]+ for 3 and m/z 495.73 [M-5I]5+, 549.20 [M-5I+3H2O]5+ for 4.

The stacking of Pcs (3,4) to the N,S-GQD through π-π stacking was achieved as represented in Fig. 1 and the loading of Pc onto N-GQDs was determined spectroscopically using UV/vis spectra as reported before [41]. The loading of Pc onto nanoparticles was as follows (mgPc/mgNP): 0.82 mg/mg for 3@N,S-GQDs and 0.59 mg/mg for 4@N,S-GQDs.

After the loading of Pcs (3,4) onto the surface of N,S-GQDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum was compared to their corresponding conjugates (3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs) in DMSO (Fig. 3 ). The Q band of the nanoconjugate is just 1 nm red shifted as compared to Pcs alone (Table 1) and the presence of this absorption band in conjugate is proof of the presence of the phthalocyanine unit. Negligible change in the Q-bands of Pcs upon coordination to carbon nanomaterials were confirmed in the previous studies [43]. The B band region was observed at 352 nm for 3@N,S-GQDs and 353 nm for 4@N,S-GQDs. The intense absorption in the high energy region (< 350 nm) in conjugate spectrum is because of the absorption of the N,S-GQDs [44] (Fig. 3).

UV–vis spectrum of the compound 4 and 4@N,S-GQDs were also studied in water with 5% DMSO to evaluate aggregation tendency in biological media. The Q-band intensity of 4 and 4@N,S-GQDs in water (5%DMSO) decreased with red shift of 6 and 5 nm, respectively, which is because of the the aggregated species

The absorption band of N,S-GQDs appeared at 336 nm ascribing the n– π* transition of CO. The band at 640 nm as weakly may be attributed to the π / π* and n / π* of CS and SO (Fig. 3a). The weak intensity of this absorption band is attributed to the low molar ratio of citric acid and thiourea [38].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted for investigation of crystallinity of the prepared N,S-GQDs, ZnPcs (3,4) before conjugation and after conjugation with N,S-GQDs to form 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs are shown in Fig. 4 . The spectrum was recorded in the 2θ angle range 10–100 °.

The XRD pattern of the N,S-GQDs displayed the presence of slightly broad peak at 2θ = 27° representing the characteristic interplanar interaction causing stacking of aromatic systems and the diffraction peak at 2θ = 17° corresponds to an interplanar structural packing. [45] The broad peak diffraction at 2θ = 46° could be attributed to the amorphous and disordered carbons in N,S-GQDs [46]. The morphology of ZnPc (3) is confirmed by the peak at 2θ = 18°. The observed broadness indicate the amorphous structure, which is typical of phthalocyanines [47]. 3@N,S-GQDs showed similar diffraction peaks at 2θ = 18° and 2θ = 35° as shifted as compared to N,S-GQDs, which is indicate the interaction between N,S-GQDs and ZnPc (3).

There are two broad diffraction peaks can be distinguished from the XRD pattern of ZnPc (4) at 2θ = 62° and 80° which is attributed to the amorphous background. Furthermore, there are several sharp diffraction peaks at between 2θ = 14° −29° which indicates the sample may undergo a crystallization process and the peaks in the low angle range, the ZnPc crystals belong to the α crystalline form [48]. Upon conjugation of N,S-GQDs and ZnPc (4), in XRD of the 4@N,S-GQDs showed similar crystalline peaks as for 4 with a slight shift to 2θ = 63° and 79° in addition to sharp diffraction peaks at between 2θ = 13° −21° as slightly shifted as compared to N,S-GQDs demonstrating that the π‐π stacking interaction of MPcs and N,S-GQDs have an effect on the structure of conjugates. Besides, from the XRD patterns, it could be confirmed that ZnPc (4) exhibited better crystallin properties than ZnPc (3)
Raman spectroscopy is utilized to determine the quality of the N,S-GQDs and their nanoconjugates with ZnPcs (3,4) are shown in Fig. 5 .

Graphene quantum dots have two diagnostic Raman peaks which are called as the G band resulting from the in-plane vibrations of sp2 carbon and D bands related to the presence of sp3 defects [49]. N,S-GQDs exhibited the D and G bands at 1325 cm−1 and 1559 cm-1, respectively. Upon conjugation, the size-dependent D and G-band frequencies shifted to higher wavenumbers at 1398 cm−1 and 1606 cm-1 for 3@N,S-GQDs and 1396 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1 for 4@N,S-GQDs. These shifts arising from the π–electron interactions between graphene and phthalocyanines are consistent with the previously observed studies [50].

The change on GQDs is measured by determining the ratio of the D over G band (D/G). The ratio of the intensities (ID/IG) of D and G bands are another important characteristic to figure the quality of the GQDs. The ID/IG ratio of the N,S-GQDs is 0.35. This indicates the ordered structure of the N,S-GQDs. Upon stacking the Pcs on to the N,S-GQDs, the observed ID/IG ratio 0.49 for 3@N,S-GQDs and 0.16 for 4@N,S-GQDs. The increased ID/IG ratio is attributed that graphene lattice becomes more disrupted [51].
As the ID/IG ratio decrease resulting from the increase in the intensity of the G band, which is associated with removal of some amorphous carbons [32].

The size of the N,S-GQDs alone and the 3@N,S-GQDs nanoconjugate were analysed by using the DLS technique (Fig. 6 ). The DLS analysis show the average size 4.6 nm for N,S-GQDs,. Upon conjugation of the graphene quantum dots to complex 3 and 4, there is an increase in the size of the nanoconjugates compared to N,S-GQDs which is attributed to aggregation [52]. Aggregation/coordination resulting from π-π stacking of the Pc (3) on N,S-GQD gave the larger size.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) was applied to consider the morphologies of N,S-GQDs alone, 3@N,S-GQDs and as shown in Fig. 7 . The TEM micrograph for N,S-GQDs showed almost monodispersed spherical shape particles with an average size of 3.8 nm (Fig. 7A). The expected aggregation behaviour was observed in the TEM micrographs upon conjugation of N,S-GQDs to ZnPcs (3,4), (Fig. 7B and C). The aggregation is always possible in phthalocyanine through π-π interaction since the MPcs might stay close to each other on quantum dots [35]. The size could not be estimated for 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs due to the aggregation unlike the monodispersed individual nanoparticles.

The elemental compositions of the N,S-GQDs alone, ZnPcs (3,4) and their conjugates (3@N,S-GQDs, 4@N,S-GQDs) were qualitatively shown by an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX) as shown in Fig. 8 . The EDX spectra for N,S-GQDs showed the presence of C, N, O and S, while ZnPc (3) and ZnPc (3) showed C, N, O, Zn and C, N, O, Zn and iodine, respectively, as expected for this Pcs. The spectrum for 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs displayed C, N, O, S, Zn and C, N, O, S, Zn, I confirming the presence of both quantum dots and the phthalocyanines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF), emission and lifetimes
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption, fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of the studied samples are shown in Fig. 9 to illustrate the measurements for compound 3 as an example.

Fluorescence behaviours of Pcs alone (3,4) and 3@N,S-GQDs, 4@N,S-GQDs were studied in DMSO and aqueous media for Pc 4 and 4@N,S-GQDs, the related data were listed in Table 1. Upon excitation at 612 nm for 3, 610 nm for 4 and 610 nm where only Pc absorbs for 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs, the fluorescence emission wavelengths were determined at 695, 699, 698 and 700 nm in DMSO, respectively.

The cationic Pc (4) and its conjugate 4@N,S-GQDs were not fluorescent in aqueous solution due to aggregation tendency of this compound in aqueous solutions and quenching effect of water (Table 1) [53]. The emission spectrum is the symmetric appearance of its absorption spectrum. This mirror image appearance of these spectra is a result of the same transitions being involved in both absorption and emission [54].

The fluorescence excitation spectrum indicates the electron distribution of the molecule in the ground state. While the excitation spectrum of 3 match the its absorption spectrum, which means all fluorescence emissions result from a same molecule, the excitation spectrum of 4, 3@N,S-GQDs and 3@N,S-GQDs are slightly red-shifted compared to its absorption spectrum, which can be attributed slightly aggregated in its ground state [55] (Table 1).

The fluorescence quantum yield ΦF of a fluorophore is the number of emitted photons via a singlet to singlet transition. The measurements were carried out with method of the comparison of the fluorescence efficiency of an unknown with a standard as reported [56,57]. Unsubstituted ZnPc was utilized as a standard with the value: ΦF = 0.2 [55].

Fluorescence quantum yield of N,S-GQDs when conjugated to ZnPc (
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
) was determined using Eq. (1):
(1)
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
=
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
F
F ( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
F
F
( G Q D s )
where Φ F(GQDs) is the fluorescence quantum yield of the GQDs alone and was used as the standard, F (GQDs) is the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs alone, and
F
F ( G Q D s )
c o n j u g a t e
is the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs coordinated to ZnPc. Fluorescence quantum yields when exciting, where GQDs absorb, were determined using quinine sulfate in H2SO4 (0.05 M) (Φ F = 0.52) [58] as the standard. The fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) of the N,S-GQDs were studied in water and found as 0.18 (Table 1).

Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a non-radiative energy transfer process occuring between a donor and an acceptor [59]. FRET efficiency (Eff) was measured experimentally from the fluorescence quantum yields of the donor (N,S-GQDs) in the absence (ΦF(GQDs)) and presence (Φconjugate F(GQDs)) of the acceptor (ZnPcs) using Eq. (2) [59]. The calculated FRET Eff is 0.82 for 3@N,S-GQDs and 0.76 for 4@N,S-GQDs.
(2)
E f f = 1 -
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
Φ
F ( G Q D s )

The fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) of the ZnPcs (3,4) and their conjugates were studied in DMSO. When the 3@N,S-GQD and 4@N,S-GQDs were excited from Pc absorption wavelength, the observed ΦF value is 0.028 and 0.025 which are lower than ZnPcs (3,4) alone with the ΦF value of 0.036 and 0.032, respectively. The decrease in the ΦF of hybrid materials compared to ZnPcs (3,4) could attributed to several possible process causing the quench of the fluorescence such as Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from a donor and acceptor molecule and inter charge transfer (ICT) between functional groups and GQDs [59]. Also, the ΦF of compound 4 and 4@N,S-GQDs measured in water (5%DMSO) couldnt be determined due to the high quenching effect of water

Fluorescence lifetime indicates the time of the absorbed photons remaining in the excited state before returning to the ground state after excitation. Fluorescence lifetime values (τF) of the studied zinc Pcs and their conjugates were measured in DMSO by TCSPC (time correlated single photon counting) method which is a counting process.

The determined τF values are 2.87 ns for 3, 2.76 for 3@N,S-GQD, 2.84 for 4 and 2.60 ns for 4@N,S-GQDs measured at the excitation wavelengths of the Pc, which are correlated with the fluorescence quantum yields (Table 1). The loading of the organic or inorganic molecules on the nanoparticles can result in increase or decrease in fluorescence lifetime. Thus, the variation in lifetime could be attributed to orientation of the complexes on the GQDs [60].
The illustrated fluorescence mono-exponential decay curve as an example with their corresponding residual graph in DMSO for complex 3 is shown in Fig. 10 .

Photodynamic process is depend on the effective interaction of the triplet state photosensitisers with ground state molecular oxygen (3O2) to form cytotoxic singlet oxygen (1O2). The measurement of 1O2 quantum yields is crucial for the evaluation the potential of the photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy applications [61].

The produced 1O2 were measured with the photochemical reaction based on the chemical quenching of DPBF and ADMA in DMSO and in aqueous media, respectively (Fig. 11 ). The progress of the reaction is monitored by the change in absorbance of the singlet oxygen quencher (DPBF/ADMA) upon light irradiation due to their photooxidation by the generated singlet oxygen in the presence of photosensitizers. The comparative method was applied with unsubstituted ZnPc (ΦΔ = 0.67 in DMSO) and AlPcSmix (containing a mixture of differently sulfonated phthalocyanines) (ΦΔ = 0.42 in aqueous media) as reference standards [62,63]. Fig. 11 shows the absorbance changes of DPBF and ADMA at various time intervals for compound 3 and 4@N,S-GQDs as an examples.

The novel ZnPc (3) bearing poli-imine substituents showed lower singlet oxygen quantum yield in DMSO with a value: ΦΔ = 0.23 compared to its quaternized derivative ZnPc (4) with a value of 0.31, due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between nitrogen-containing substituents and phthalocyanine ring. After quaternization, the bonding of the lone pair electrons of nitrogen atoms to methyl groups could cause to prevent the intramolecular PET process [64]. The ΦΔ values of the 3@N,S-GQDs with 0.32 and 4@N,S-GQDs with 0.42 are higher than that of alone ZnPcs (3,4). The increase in ΦΔ corresponds to the increase in triplet state populations. Comparing the ΦΔ values for 4 and 4@N,S-GQDs in DMSO and water, the smaller value in water is because of the aggregation tendency of 4 in aqueous media and the quenching of singlet oxygen by water compared to that in DMSO (Table 1)

The triplet quantum yields (ΦT) indicate triplet population of the excited molecules. The triplet quantum yield (ΦT) and triplet lifetimes values of the Pcs alone and Pcs@N,S-GQDs were determined using comparative methods defined in the literature using a laser flash photolysis system by employing the unsubstituted ZnPc as a standard in DMSO (ΦT = 0.65) [63]. The ΦT and τT recorded in DMSO at 676 nm cross-over wavelength are summarized in Table 1. The triplet decay curve of complex 3 is shown in Fig. 12 as an example.

The zinc Pcs alone (3,4) and Pc-conjugate graphene quantum dots 3@N,S-GQDs, 4@N,S-GQDs displayed high triplet quantum yield (ΦT) with the value of 0.54, 0.56, 0.61 and 0.65, respectively. Conjugation of the ZnPcs to N,S-GQD resulted in more efficient intersystem crossing to the triplet state, resulting in the more populated triplet excited photosensitizer in this state. The triplet quantum yield (ΦT) values followed the same action with singlet oxygen quantum yield values. A high triplet quantum yield transform molecular oxygen into high singlet oxygen. High triplet quantum yield (ΦT) values exhibit the low triplet lifetimes [65]. The triplet lifetimes were observed 346 μs for 3, 321 μs for 3@N,S-GQDs, 338 μs for 4 and 317 μs for 4@N,S-GQDs (Table 1).

Antibacterial efficiency of PDT is considered a promising alternative to other kinds of antibiotic treatments. PACT is independent of bacteria resistance model to antibiotics and demonstrates fast inactivation than usual antimicrobials [66].

We prepared poli-imine substituted ZnPc (3) and its positively charge drivative ZnPc (4) conjugated to nitrogen, sulfur co-doped GQDs (3@N,S-GQDs, 4@N,S-GQDs) as photosensitizers to measure the PACT activity. The photodynamic activities of the compounds were studied against gram positive S. Aureus.

The samples were applied 10 μM concentrations (based on Pc) of complex/conjugate for 3, 3@N,S-GQDs and 0.5 μM for 4, 4@N,S-GQDs in water (5% DMSO) (Table 2 ). Plate count method was applied. That is the most commonly used method for the determination of bacterial populations and evaluating antibacterial properties of the substances by allowing for growth and formation of colonies on solid media [21].

The PACT activity were quantified as percentage reductions/viable cells and logarithmic reduction values (Fig. 13 , Table 2). Control experiments also were carried out and it was seen the viability of bacteria was insignificantly affected by illumination (data not shown) in the absence of photosensitizers.

Log reductions were calculated by using Eq. 2 to determine the reduction of viable microorganisms using a logarithmic scale [67],
(3) log reduction = log10(A) − log10(B)
where A and B are the number of viable micro-organism, respectively before and after treatment. Percentage reductions is a ratio of the number of viable colonies after treatment with the photosensitizer to viable colonies of the before treatment

The survival percentage (viable colonies) of the bacteria is express the number of alive colonies after treatment of the cells. Fig. 13 represents the Logarithmic reduction (a) and bar graph (b) of survival percentage of S. aureus performed with light and in dark for compounds.

ZnPc (3) showed high photocytotoxicities against S. Aureus after irridiation for 20 min intervals up to 80 min resulted in a reduction of 3.53 log10 corresponding to 99.91 % reduction of bacteria cells (Fig. 13b). The obtained nanoconjugate has better activity to that of unconjugated Pc alone. The application of 3@N,S-GQD led to a complete cell death after 80 min irridation (Fig. 13a).

The efficiency of bacteria photodynamic inactivation depends on the properties of photosensitizers molecules. Pcs carrying positively charged substituents are promising in order to increase solubility in physiological media following enhancing the photosensitization efficiency towards the bacteria [68]. In this study, the tested photosensitiser binding poli-cationic substituents and its conjugate 4@N,S-GQDs had high efficiency against S. Aureus, which were completely inactivated with very low concentrations of photosensitiser (0.5 μM (based on Pc)) for 40 min and 20 min irridations with corresponding reduction of 9.68 log10 value, respectively.

The enhanced activities of 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs compared to Pcs alone (3,4) could be due to high ability of GQDs binding to the cellular membranes. GQDs may disrupt the membrane and cause canal large enough to allow PS to enter cytoplasm. After entering the cell, they might prevents protein synthesis and DNA replication in addition to generating singlet oxygen [69]. Besides, as the singlet oxygen production increased for the 3@N,S-GQDs and 4@N,S-GQDs compared to ZnPcs (3,4) alone, photoactivity resulted in the complete cell destruction as seen in Fig. 14 showing the existence of the bacteria colonies before and after treatment.

The measurements were carried out also in the dark environment assessing the same bacterial an PS solutions to see the bactericidal effect of the PS without light. As demonstrated in Fig. 13, all of the studied samples had negligible dark toxicity on S. Aureus.

The new ZnPcs (3,4) bearing octa-imine and their nanoconjugates (3@N,S-GQDs, 4@N,S-GQDs) were synthesized and characterized in this study. The photophysical and photochemical measurements were evaluated. When N,S-GQDs were combined with ZnPcs (3,4), singlet oxygen generation increased. in vitro photodynamic antibacterial studies towards Staphylococcus aureus as gram positive bacteria were carried out. When nitrogen, sulphur co-doped GQDs are combined with Pc (3,4), same amount of Pc produced more anti-bacterial activity. 3@N,S-GQDs, 4 and 4@N,S-GQDs proved to be an efficient PSs for the photodynamic inactivation with the 100 % reduction percentage. In the light of the obtained results, we can conclude that especially the 4 and 4@N,S-GQDs can inhibit S. Aureus in a short time irridation and at very low concentrations in hospitals, in waste waters, in localized human infections in dermatology, and open wounds and burns as promising drug for antibacterial applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation, National Research Foundation (NRF) South Africa and Sasol Inzalo Foundation through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Howard parish, stanley B. brown, photoinactivation of bacteria. Use of a cationic water-soluble zinc phthalocyanine to photoinactivate both gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Andrew Minnock, David I. Vernon, Jack Schofield, John Griffiths. Howard Parish, Stanley B. Brown, Photoinactivation of bacteria. Use of a cationic water-soluble zinc phthalocyanine to photoinactivate both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology 32 (1996) 159-164
Kholikova K, Ilhoma S, Sajjada M, Smith ME, Monroe JD, Sanc O, Era AO (2018) Photodiagnosis and Photodynamic Therapy 24:7–14.
